narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw such a high risk—95% chance—of you struggling to pass the bar exam, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: your first-year grades landed you in the bottom decile of your school, the single strongest signal that whispered early struggles with the rigors of legal study. That pattern held steady by your third year, still in the bottom decile, reinforcing the model's worry that academic hurdles might persist right through bar prep. Your LSAT score of 26, while a real accomplishment in its own right, sat on the lower side compared to peers who typically thrive, pulling the prediction firmly toward failure as the third-biggest factor. Even your upper-middle family income, which can sometimes offer a safety net, played into the model's caution here, suggesting it hasn't yet bridged those performance gaps. As an Asian female going full-time, those traits layered on subtle statistical risks the model weighed heavily, alongside your undergrad GPA of 2.8, which echoed the same theme of needing a stronger foundation. Every piece—your deciles, scores, background, and plans—wove into a picture of someone the model fears could face an uphill battle against the bar's demands. It's not about your potential or passion; it's this specific combination that lit up red flags based on patterns from thousands of similar journeys. The good news? This is just one data-driven snapshot—you can retake the LSAT, rebuild those grades, or find programs that spotlight your unique strengths. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours. With encouragement, [Your Guide]
